(**IndE**,[[4]](#cite_note-4) **IE**) or **"English (India)" (en-IN)** is a group of [dialects](/wiki/Dialect "Dialect") of the [English language](/wiki/English_language "English language") spoken in the [Republic of India](/wiki/India "India") and among the [Indian diaspora](/wiki/Indian_diaspora "Indian diaspora") and is native to India.[[5]](#cite_note-English_in_India-5) English is one of the two languages used by the [Government of India](/wiki/Government_of_India "Government of India") for communication, and is enshrined in the [Constitution of India](/wiki/Constitution_of_India "Constitution of India").[[6]](#cite_note-Languages_in_the_Constitution_of_India-6) English is also an [official language](/wiki/Languages_with_official_status_in_India "Languages with official status in India") in eight states and seven [union territories](/wiki/Union_territories "Union territories") of India, and the additional official language in five other [states](/wiki/States_of_India "States of India") and one union territory. [India](/wiki/India "India") has one of the world’s largest [English-speaking communities](/wiki/English-speaking_world "English-speaking world").[[7]](#cite_note-7) Furthermore, English is the sole official language of the [Judiciary of India](/wiki/Judiciary_of_India "Judiciary of India"), unless the state [governor](/wiki/Governor_(India) "Governor (India)") or legislature mandates the use of a regional language, or if the [President of India](/wiki/President_of_India "President of India") has given approval for the use of regional languages in courts.[[8]](#cite_note-The_Economic_Times-8)

**Indian English** (**IndE**,[[4]](#cite_note-4) **IE**) or **"English (India)" (en-IN)** is a group of [dialects](/wiki/Dialect "Dialect") of the [English language](/wiki/English_language "English language") spoken in the [Republic of India](/wiki/India "India") and among the [Indian diaspora](/wiki/Indian_diaspora "Indian diaspora") and is native to India.[[5]](#cite_note-English_in_India-5) English is one of the two languages used by the [Government of India](/wiki/Government_of_India "Government of India") for communication, and is enshrined in the [Constitution of India](/wiki/Constitution_of_India "Constitution of India").[[6]](#cite_note-Languages_in_the_Constitution_of_India-6) English is also an [official language](/wiki/Languages_with_official_status_in_India "Languages with official status in India") in eight states and seven [union territories](/wiki/Union_territories "Union territories") of India, and the additional official language in five other [states](/wiki/States_of_India "States of India") and one union territory. [India](/wiki/India "India") has one of the world’s largest [English-speaking communities](/wiki/English-speaking_world "English-speaking world").[[7]](#cite_note-7) Furthermore, English is the sole official language of the [Judiciary of India](/wiki/Judiciary_of_India "Judiciary of India"), unless the state [governor](/wiki/Governor_(India) "Governor (India)") or legislature mandates the use of a regional language, or if the [President of India](/wiki/President_of_India "President of India") has given approval for the use of regional languages in courts.[[8]](#cite_note-The_Economic_Times-8)

Before the dissolution of the [British Empire](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire") on the [Indian subcontinent](/wiki/Indian_subcontinent "Indian subcontinent"), the term *Indian English* broadly referred to *[South Asian English](/wiki/South_Asian_English "South Asian English")*, also known as *[British Indian](/wiki/British_Raj "British Raj") English*.

## Status

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indian_English&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Status")]

After gaining [independence](/wiki/Indian_Independence_Act_1947 "Indian Independence Act 1947") from the [British Raj](/wiki/British_Raj "British Raj") in 1947, English remained an official language of the new [Dominion of India](/wiki/Dominion_of_India "Dominion of India") and later the [Republic of India](/wiki/Republic_of_India "Republic of India"). After the [partition of India](/wiki/Partition_of_India "Partition of India"), [Pakistani English](/wiki/Pakistani_English "Pakistani English") and [Bangladeshi English](/wiki/Bangladeshi_English "Bangladeshi English") were considered separate from Indian English.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In the 21st century, only a few hundred thousand Indians, or less than 0.1% of the total population, report English as their first language,[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10)[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12) and around 30% of the [Indian population](/wiki/Demographics_of_India "Demographics of India") can speak English to some extent.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

According to the [2001 Census](/wiki/2001_Census_of_India "2001 Census of India"), 12.18% of Indians knew English at that time. Of those, approximately 200,000 reported that it was their first language, 86 million reported that it was their second, and 39 million reported that it was their third.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

According to the 2005 [India Human Development Survey](/wiki/India_Human_Development_Survey "India Human Development Survey"),[[15]](#cite_note-15) of 41,554 surveyed, households reported that 72% of men (29,918) spoke no English, 28% of them (11,635) spoke at least some English, and 5% of them (2,077, roughly 17.9% of those who spoke at least some English) spoke fluent English. Among women, 83% (34,489) spoke no English, 17% (7,064) spoke at least some English, and 3% (1,246, roughly 17.6% of those who spoke at least some English) spoke English fluently.[[16]](#cite_note-16) According to statistics from the District Information System for Education (DISE) of the [National University of Educational Planning and Administration](/wiki/National_University_of_Educational_Planning_and_Administration "National University of Educational Planning and Administration") under the [Ministry of Human Resource Development](/wiki/Ministry_of_Human_Resource_Development "Ministry of Human Resource Development"), [Government of India](/wiki/Government_of_India "Government of India"), enrollment in English-medium schools increased by 50% between 2008–09 and 2013–14. The number of English-medium school students in India increased from over 15 million in 2008–09 to 29 million by 2013–14.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

According to the [2011 Census](/wiki/2011_Census_of_India "2011 Census of India"), 129 million Indians (10.6%) spoke English. 259,678 (0.02%) Indians reported English as their first language.[[1]](#cite_note-auto-1) It concluded that approximately 83 million Indians (6.8%) reported English as their second language, and 46 million (3.8%) reported it as their third language, making English the second-most spoken language in India.[[2]](#cite_note-censusindia.gov.in-2)

India ranks 52 out of 111 countries in the 2022 [EF English Proficiency Index](/wiki/EF_English_Proficiency_Index "EF English Proficiency Index") published by the [EF Education First](/wiki/EF_Education_First "EF Education First"). The index gives the country a score of 496 indicating "moderate proficiency". India ranks 6th out of 24 Asian countries included in the index.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

As a [multilingual](/wiki/Multilingualism "Multilingualism") country, English is the *[lingua franca](/wiki/Lingua_franca "Lingua franca")* among different regions of India.[[19]](#cite_note-19) Writing for *[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")*, journalist [Manu Joseph](/wiki/Manu_Joseph "Manu Joseph") stated in 2011 that, due to the prominence and usage of the language and the desire for English-language education, "English is the *[de facto](/wiki/De_facto "De facto")* national language of India. It is a bitter truth."[[20]](#cite_note-20) In his book, *In Search of Indian English: History, Politics and Indigenisation*, Ranjan Kumar Auddy shows that the history of the rise of [Indian nationalism](/wiki/Indian_nationalism "Indian nationalism") and the history of the emergence of Indian English are deeply inter-related.[[21]](#cite_note-21) [Purdue University](/wiki/Purdue_University "Purdue University") stated due to the prevalence of the language in business operations in India, it is "uncommon to find a business enterprise using non-English documents".[[22]](#cite_note-22)

### Court language

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indian_English&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Court language")]

Under the Indian Constitution, English is the language of [India's Supreme Court](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_India "Supreme Court of India") and of all the [high courts of India](/wiki/High_courts_of_India "High courts of India").[[8]](#cite_note-The_Economic_Times-8) However, as allowed by the Constitution, Hindi is also used in courts in [Bihar](/wiki/Bihar "Bihar"), [Madhya Pradesh](/wiki/Madhya_Pradesh "Madhya Pradesh"), [Uttar Pradesh](/wiki/Uttar_Pradesh "Uttar Pradesh"), and [Rajasthan](/wiki/Rajasthan "Rajasthan") by virtue of special presidential approval.[[23]](#cite_note-23) As of 2018[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Indian_English&action=edit), the high courts of [Punjab](/wiki/Punjab_(Indian_state) "Punjab (Indian state)") and [Haryana](/wiki/Haryana "Haryana") were also awaiting presidential approval to use Hindi alongside English,[[24]](#cite_note-24) and the [Madras High Court](/wiki/Madras_High_Court "Madras High Court") has been taking steps to use [Tamil](/wiki/Tamil_language "Tamil language") alongside English.[[25]](#cite_note-25)

## Names

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indian_English&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Names")]

The first occurrence of the term *Indian English* dates from 1696,[[26]](#cite_note-26) though the term did not become common until the 19th century. In the colonial era, the most common terms in use were *Anglo-Indian English*, or simply *Anglo-Indian*, both dating from 1860. Other less common terms in use were *Indo-Anglian* (dating from 1897) and *Indo-English* (1912).[[27]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-27) An item of Anglo-Indian English was known as an *Anglo-Indianism* from 1851.[[27]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-27)

In the modern era, a range of colloquial [portmanteau](/wiki/Portmanteau "Portmanteau") words for Indian English have been used. The earliest of these is *Indlish* (recorded from 1962), and others include *Indiglish* (1974), *Indenglish* (1979), *Indglish* (1984), *Indish* (1984), *Inglish* (1985) and *Indianlish* (2007).[[28]](#cite_note-28) Sometimes, Indian English is also referred to as Macaulay's English after [Thomas Babington Macaulay](/wiki/Thomas_Babington_Macaulay "Thomas Babington Macaulay").[[29]](#cite_note-29)

## Features

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indian_English&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Features")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [[icon]](/wiki/File:Wiki_letter_w_cropped.svg) | This section **needs expansion**. You can help by [adding missing information](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Indian_English&action=edit&section=). *(December 2019)* |

Indian English generally uses the [Indian numbering system](/wiki/Indian_numbering_system "Indian numbering system"). Idiomatic forms derived from Indian literary languages and vernaculars have been absorbed into Indian English. Nevertheless, there remains general homogeneity in phonetics, vocabulary, and phraseology among various dialects of Indian English.[[30]](#cite_note-30)[[31]](#cite_note-31)[[32]](#cite_note-32)[[33]](#cite_note-33)

Formal written publications in English in India tend to use [lakh](/wiki/Lakh "Lakh")/[crore](/wiki/Crore "Crore") for [Indian currency](/wiki/Indian_rupee "Indian rupee") and Western numbering for foreign currencies like [dollars](/wiki/United_States_dollar "United States dollar") and [pounds](/wiki/Pound_sterling "Pound sterling"),[[34]](#cite_note-34) although lakh and crore are also used to refer to other large numbers such as population sizes. These terms are not used by other English-speakers, who have to learn what they mean in order to read Indian English news articles.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indian_English&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: History")]

See also: [Glossary of the British Raj](/wiki/Glossary_of_the_British_Raj "Glossary of the British Raj")

### British India

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indian_English&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: British India")]

The English language established a foothold on the [Indian subcontinent](/wiki/Indian_subcontinent "Indian subcontinent") with the granting of the [East India Company](/wiki/East_India_Company "East India Company") charter by [Queen Elizabeth I](/wiki/Queen_Elizabeth_I "Queen Elizabeth I") in 1600 and the subsequent establishment of trading ports in coastal cities such as [Surat](/wiki/Surat "Surat"), [Mumbai](/wiki/Mumbai "Mumbai") (called Bombay before 1995), [Chennai](/wiki/Chennai "Chennai") (called Madras before 1996), and [Kolkata](/wiki/Kolkata "Kolkata") (called Calcutta before 2001).

English-language public instruction began in the subcontinent in the 1830s during the [rule of the British East India Company](/wiki/Company_rule_in_India#Education "Company rule in India"). In 1835, English [replaced](/wiki/English_Education_Act_1835 "English Education Act 1835") [Persian](/wiki/Persian_language "Persian language") as the official language of the East India Company. [Lord Macaulay](/wiki/Thomas_Babington_Macaulay,_1st_Baron_Macaulay "Thomas Babington Macaulay, 1st Baron Macaulay") played a major role in introducing English and Western concepts into educational institutions in British-India. He supported the replacement of Persian by English as the official language, the use of English as the medium of instruction in all schools, and the training of English-speaking Indians as teachers.[[35]](#cite_note-John_MacKenzie_2013-35) Throughout the 1840s and 1850s, primary, middle, and high schools were opened in many districts of [British India](/wiki/Presidencies_and_provinces_of_British_India "Presidencies and provinces of British India"), with most high schools offering English language instruction in some subjects. In 1857, just before the end of East India Company rule, universities that were modeled on the [University of London](/wiki/University_of_London "University of London") and used English as the medium of instruction were established in Bombay, Calcutta and Madras. During the [British Raj](/wiki/British_Raj "British Raj") (1858 to 1947), English-language penetration increased throughout the subcontinent. This was driven in part by the gradually increasing hiring of Indians in the [civil services](/wiki/Civil_services "Civil services"). At the time of [Indian independence](/wiki/Indian_Independence_Act_1947 "Indian Independence Act 1947") in 1947, English was the only functional *[lingua franca](/wiki/Lingua_franca "Lingua franca")* in the region.

The [Oxford English Dictionary](/wiki/Oxford_English_Dictionary "Oxford English Dictionary") stated that in this period, "English ceased to be a language used solely by foreigners in India, and became the dominant form of communication in Indian education, government, commerce, literature, and print media."[[36]](#cite_note-36)

### Republic of India

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indian_English&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Republic of India")]

After the independence and [Partition of British India](/wiki/Partition_of_British_India "Partition of British India"), [Modern Standard Hindi](/wiki/Modern_Standard_Hindi "Modern Standard Hindi") was declared the first official language in the new Indian Republic, and attempts were made to declare Hindi the sole national language. Due to [protests from Tamil Nadu](/wiki/Anti-Hindi_agitations_of_Tamil_Nadu "Anti-Hindi agitations of Tamil Nadu") and other non-Hindi-speaking states, it was decided to temporarily retain English for official purposes until at least 1965. By the end of this period, however, opposition from non-Hindi states was still too strong to have Hindi declared the sole language. With this in mind, the English Language Amendment Bill declared English to be an associate language "until such time as all non-Hindi States had agreed to its being dropped."[[37]](#cite_note-37) This has not yet occurred, and English is still widely used. For instance, it is the only reliable means of day-to-day communication between the central government and the non-Hindi states.

The view of the [English language](/wiki/English_language "English language") among many Indians has changed over time. It used to be associated primarily with colonialism; it is now primarily associated with economic progress, and English continues to be an official language of India.[[38]](#cite_note-38) Indian men who speak fluent English have been found to earn 34% higher hourly salaries than men who don't speak English.[[39]](#cite_note-39)

While there is an assumption that English is readily available in India, studies show that its usage is actually restricted to the elite,[[40]](#cite_note-40) because of inadequate [education](/wiki/Education_in_India "Education in India") to large parts of the Indian population. It has been suggested that Indian English, rather than British English, should be taught in schools, to allow for international cooperation while valuing local cultural features "due to a set of unique lexical, grammatical, phonological and discourse features that would allow it to act as both a lingua franca within the country and on the international stage".[[41]](#cite_note-41) The use of outdated teaching methods and the poor grasp of English exhibited by the authors of many guidebooks disadvantage students who rely on these books, giving India only a moderate proficiency in English.[[42]](#cite_note-42)

In addition, many features of Indian English were imported into [Bhutan](/wiki/Bhutan "Bhutan") due to the dominance of Indian-style education and teachers in the country after it withdrew from its isolation in the 1960s.[[43]](#cite_note-43)[[44]](#cite_note-44)

## Hinglish and other hybrid languages

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indian_English&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Hinglish and other hybrid languages")]

Main articles: [Hinglish](/wiki/Hinglish "Hinglish"), [Tenglish](/wiki/Tenglish "Tenglish"), and [Tanglish](/wiki/Tanglish "Tanglish")

See also: [Englishisation § South Asia](/wiki/Englishisation#South_Asia "Englishisation")

The term *Hinglish* is a [portmanteau](/wiki/Portmanteau "Portmanteau") of the languages [English](/wiki/English_language "English language") and [Hindi](/wiki/Hindi "Hindi"). This typically refers to the [macaronic](/wiki/Macaronic_language "Macaronic language") hybrid use of [Hindustani](/wiki/Hindustani_language "Hindustani language") and English. It is often the growing preferred language of the urban and semi-urban educated Indian youth, as well as the Indian diaspora abroad.[[45]](#cite_note-The_Times_of_India-45) The Hindi film industry, more popularly known as [Bollywood](/wiki/Bollywood "Bollywood"), incorporates considerable amounts of Hinglish as well.[[46]](#cite_note-46) Many internet platforms and voice commands on Google also recognise Hinglish.[[45]](#cite_note-The_Times_of_India-45) When [Hindi](/wiki/Hindi "Hindi")–[Urdu](/wiki/Urdu "Urdu") is viewed as a single language called [Hindustani](/wiki/Hindustani_language "Hindustani language"), the [portmanteaus](/wiki/Portmanteau "Portmanteau") Hinglish and [Urdish](/wiki/Urdish "Urdish") mean the same [code-mixed tongue](/wiki/Code-mixing "Code-mixing"), where the former term is used predominantly in modern India and the latter term predominantly in [Pakistan](/wiki/Pakistan "Pakistan").

Other macaronic hybrids such as *Minglish* ([Marathi](/wiki/Marathi_language "Marathi language") and English), *[Banglish](/wiki/Banglish "Banglish")* ([Bengali](/wiki/Bengali_language "Bengali language") and English), *[Manglish](/wiki/Manglish_(Malayalam_and_English) "Manglish (Malayalam and English)")* ([Malayalam](/wiki/Malayalam_language "Malayalam language") and English), *[Kanglish](/wiki/Kanglish "Kanglish")* ([Kannada](/wiki/Kannada_language "Kannada language") and English), *[Tenglish](/wiki/Tenglish "Tenglish")* ([Telugu](/wiki/Telugu_language "Telugu language") and English), and *[Tanglish](/wiki/Tanglish "Tanglish")* or *Tamglish* ([Tamil](/wiki/Tamil_language "Tamil language") and English) exist in South India.[[47]](#cite_note-47)

## Phonology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indian_English&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Phonology")]

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indian_English&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Vowels")]

* North Indians, especially a minority of English students and teachers along with some people in various professions like telephone customer service agents, often speak with a [non-rhotic](/wiki/Rhoticity_in_English "Rhoticity in English") accent. Examples of this include *flower* pronounced as [flaʊ.ə], *never* as [nevə], *water* as [ʋɒtə], etc. Some South Indians, such as native Telugu speakers, speak with a rhotic accent, but the final /ə/ becomes an [a], and an [alveolar tap](/wiki/Alveolar_tap "Alveolar tap") [ɾ] is used for /r/, resulting in *water* and *never* as [wɒtaɾ] or [ʋɒʈaɾ] and [nevaɾ] respectively.
  + Features characteristic of [North American English](/wiki/North_American_English "North American English"), such as rhoticity and [r-coloured vowels](/wiki/R-colored_vowel "R-colored vowel"), have been gaining influence on Indian English in recent years as cultural and economic ties increase between India and the United States.[[51]](#cite_note-:0-51)
* Many North Indians have an [intonation pattern](/wiki/Intonation_pattern "Intonation pattern") similar to [Hiberno-English](/wiki/Hiberno-English "Hiberno-English"), which perhaps results from a similar pattern used while speaking Hindi.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

#### Splits and mergers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indian_English&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Splits and mergers")]

* Indian English speakers do not necessarily make a clear distinction between [/ɒ/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") and [/ɔː/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") unlike [Received Pronunciation](/wiki/Received_Pronunciation "Received Pronunciation") (RP), i.e. they may have the [cot-caught merger](/wiki/Cot-caught_merger "Cot-caught merger"), with the target vowel ranging between either option.
* Most Indians have the [trap–bath split](/wiki/Trap%E2%80%93bath_split "Trap–bath split") of Received Pronunciation, affecting words such as *class*, *staff* and *last* (/klɑːs/, /stɑːf/ and /lɑːst/ respectively). Though the trap-bath split is prevalent in Indian English, it varies greatly. Many younger Indians who read and listen to [American English](/wiki/American_English "American English") do not have this split.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Similar to [Australian English](/wiki/Australian_English "Australian English"), variability is especially present when the split occurs before nasal clusters in words such as *dance*, *Francis*, and *answer*.[[52]](#cite_note-:2-52)
* Most[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Indians do not have the [hoarse-horse merger](/wiki/English-language_vowel_changes_before_historic_/r/ "English-language vowel changes before historic /r/").

The following are the standard variations in Indian English:

* The voiceless [plosives](/wiki/Stop_consonant "Stop consonant") /p/, /t/, /k/ are always unaspirated in Indian English, (aspirated in cultivated form) whereas in RP, General American and most other English accents they are aspirated in word-initial or stressed syllables. Thus "pin" is pronounced [pɪn] in Indian English but [pʰɪn] in most other dialects. In native Indo-Aryan languages, a predominant language family in India, the distinction between aspirated and unaspirated plosives is phonemic, and the English stops are equated with the unaspirated rather than the aspirated phonemes of the local languages.[[53]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWells1982627–628-53) The same is true of the voiceless postalveolar affricate /tʃ/. The local unvoiced aspirated plosives are instead equated with English fricatives, namely /f/ and /θ/.
* The [alveolar](/wiki/Alveolar_consonant "Alveolar consonant") stops English /d/, /t/ are often [retroflex](/wiki/Retroflex_consonant "Retroflex consonant") [ɖ], [ʈ], especially in the north of India.[[54]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWells1982628-54) In Indian languages, there are two entirely distinct sets of coronal plosives: one [dental](/wiki/Dental_consonant "Dental consonant") and the other retroflex. Native speakers of Indian languages prefer to pronounce the English alveolar plosives sound as more retroflex than dental,[[55]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBallMuller2014-55) and the use of retroflex consonants is a common feature of Indian English.[[56]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBallMuller2014289b-56)[[57]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESailaja2007252-57) In the [Devanagari](/wiki/Devanagari "Devanagari") script of Hindi, all alveolar plosives of English are transcribed as their retroflex counterparts. One good reason for this is that unlike most other native Indian languages, Hindi does not have *true* retroflex plosives (Tiwari, [1955] 2001). The so-called retroflexes in Hindi are actually articulated as apical [post-alveolar](/wiki/Postalveolar_consonant "Postalveolar consonant") plosives, sometimes even with a tendency to come down to the alveolar region. So a Hindi speaker normally cannot distinguish the difference between their own apical post-alveolar plosives and English's alveolar plosives. Languages such as [Tamil](/wiki/Tamil_language "Tamil language") and [Malayalam](/wiki/Malayalam "Malayalam") have *true* retroflex plosives, however, wherein the articulation is done with the tongue curved upwards and backwards at the [roof of the mouth](/wiki/Palate "Palate"). This also causes (in parts of [Uttar Pradesh](/wiki/Uttar_Pradesh "Uttar Pradesh"), [Madhya Pradesh](/wiki/Madhya_Pradesh "Madhya Pradesh") and [Bihar](/wiki/Bihar "Bihar")) the /s/ preceding alveolar /t/ to allophonically change to [ʃ] (⟨stop⟩, /stɒp/ → /ʃʈap/). Mostly in north India, some speakers allophonically further change the voiced retroflex plosives to voiced [retroflex flap](/wiki/Retroflex_flap "Retroflex flap") [ɽ], and the nasal /n/ to a nasalised retroflex flap. Among [Malayalam](/wiki/Malayalam "Malayalam") speakers, the realisation of English /t/ follows systematic rules. Word-initial /t/ is typically realised as retroflex [ʈ], while in non-initial positions it may be either alveolar [t] or retroflex [ʈ], depending on word structure. For example, better is pronounced [bettɐr] (alveolar), whereas butter is pronounced [bɐʈʈɐr] (retroflex). This is influenced by the fact that Malayalam already contrasts alveolar and retroflex plosives.
* Most major native languages of India lack the dental fricatives [/θ/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") and [/ð/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") (spelled with *th*), although [ð] occurs variably as [intervocalic](/wiki/Intervocalic_consonant "Intervocalic consonant") allophones in [Gujarati](/wiki/Gujarati_phonology "Gujarati phonology")[[58]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECardonaSuthar2003665-58) and [Tamil](/wiki/Tamil_phonology "Tamil phonology"). Usually, the [aspirated](/wiki/Aspiration_(phonetics) "Aspiration (phonetics)") [voiceless dental plosive](/wiki/Voiceless_dental_plosive "Voiceless dental plosive") [t̪ʰ] is substituted for /θ/ in the north (it would be unaspirated in the south) and the unaspirated [voiced dental plosive](/wiki/Voiced_dental_plosive "Voiced dental plosive") [d̪], or possibly the aspirated version [d̪ʱ], is substituted for /ð/.[[59]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWells1982629-59) For example, "thin" would be realised as [t̪ʰɪn] instead of /θɪn/ for North Indian speakers, whereas it would be pronounced unaspirated in the south.
* The English of [Delhi](/wiki/Delhi "Delhi") often has [yod-dropping](/wiki/Yod-dropping "Yod-dropping") after coronals, unlike RP.[[52]](#cite_note-:2-52)
* The [rhotic consonant](/wiki/Rhotic_consonant "Rhotic consonant") /r/ is pronounced by most speakers as an [alveolar tap](/wiki/Alveolar_tap "Alveolar tap") [ɾ], but may also be pronounced as a retroflex flap [ɽ] or [alveolar trill](/wiki/Alveolar_trill "Alveolar trill") [r] based on the influence by the native phonology, or an [alveolar approximant](/wiki/Alveolar_and_postalveolar_approximants "Alveolar and postalveolar approximants") [ɹ] like in most varieties of English.[[60]](#cite_note-60)[[61]](#cite_note-61)
* Indian English is variably rhotic; with pronunciations either being non-rhotic due to the traditional influence of RP, or generally rhotic due to the underlying [phonotactics](/wiki/Phonotactics "Phonotactics") of the native [Indo-Aryan](/wiki/Indo-Aryan_languages "Indo-Aryan languages") and [Dravidian](/wiki/Dravidian_languages "Dravidian languages") languages.[[62]](#cite_note-62)[[59]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWells1982629-59)
  + In recent years, rhoticity has been increasing.[[59]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWells1982629-59) Generally, [American English](/wiki/American_English "American English") is seen as having a large influence on the English language in India recently.[[51]](#cite_note-:0-51)
  + Many Indians with rhotic accents prefer to pronounce words with [aʊə] as [aː(r)], such as ⟨flower⟩ as [flaː(r)] and ⟨our⟩ as [aː(r)], as opposed to [flaʊ.ə] and [aʊ.ə] in more non-rhotic varieties. Speakers with rhotic accents, especially some south Indians, may also pronounce word-final /ər/ as /ar/, resulting in *water* and *never* as /wɔːtar/ and /nevar/ respectively.
* Most Indian languages do not differentiate between /v/ ([voiced labiodental fricative](/wiki/Voiced_labiodental_fricative "Voiced labiodental fricative")) and /w/ ([voiced labial–velar approximant](/wiki/Voiced_labial%E2%80%93velar_approximant "Voiced labial–velar approximant")). Instead, many Indians use a frictionless [voiced labiodental approximant](/wiki/Voiced_labiodental_approximant "Voiced labiodental approximant") [ʋ] for words with either sound, possibly in free variation with [v] and/or [w] depending upon region. Thus, *wet* and *vet* are often homophones.[[63]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWells1982627-63)
* South Indians tend to curl the tongue (retroflex accentuation) less for /l/ and /n/.
* Sometimes, Indian speakers interchange /s/ and /z/, especially when plurals are being formed, unlike speakers of other varieties of English, who use [s] for the pluralisation of words ending in a voiceless consonant, [z] for words ending in a voiced consonant or vowel, and [ɨz] for words ending in a sibilant.
* In case of the postalveolar affricates /tʃ/ /dʒ/, native languages like Hindi have corresponding affricates articulated from the palatal region, rather than postalveolar, and they have more of a stop component than fricative; this is reflected in their English.
* [Syllabic](/wiki/Syllabic_consonant "Syllabic consonant") /l/, /m/ and /n/ are usually replaced by the VC clusters [əl], [əm] and [ən] (as in *button* /ˈbəʈən/), or if a [high vowel](/wiki/Close_vowel "Close vowel") precedes, by [il] (as in *little* /ˈliʈil/). Syllable nuclei in words with the spelling *er*/*re* (a [schwa](/wiki/%C6%8F "Ə") in RP and an r-coloured schwa in [GA](/wiki/General_American_English "General American English")) are also replaced by VC clusters. e.g., *metre*, /ˈmiːtər/ → /ˈmiːʈər/.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
* Indian English uses clear [l] in all instances like [Irish English](/wiki/Hiberno-English "Hiberno-English") whereas other varieties use clear [l] in syllable-initial positions and [dark l](/wiki/Voiced_dental_and_alveolar_lateral_approximants "Voiced dental and alveolar lateral approximants") [[ɫ](/wiki/Velarized_alveolar_lateral_approximant "Velarized alveolar lateral approximant")] (velarised-L) in coda and syllabic positions.
* Voiced postalveolar fricative [[ʒ](/wiki/Voiced_postalveolar_fricative "Voiced postalveolar fricative")] is largely nonexistent in most varieties.[[64]](#cite_note-64)

The following are variations in Indian English due to [language contact](/wiki/Language_contact "Language contact") with Indian languages:

* Most Indian languages (except [Assamese](/wiki/Assamese_language "Assamese language"), [Kashmiri](/wiki/Kashmiri_language "Kashmiri language"), [Marathi](/wiki/Marathi_language "Marathi language"), [Tamil](/wiki/Tamil_language "Tamil language") and [Urdu](/wiki/Urdu "Urdu"); and conscious pronunciation by [Hindi](/wiki/Hindi "Hindi"), [Punjabi](/wiki/Punjabi_language "Punjabi language"), [Dogri](/wiki/Dogri_language "Dogri language"), etc. speakers) lack the [voiced alveolar fricative](/wiki/Voiced_alveolar_fricative "Voiced alveolar fricative") /z/. A significant portion of Indians thus, even though their native languages do have its nearest equivalent: the unvoiced /s/, often use the voiced palatal affricate (or postalveolar) /dʒ/. This makes words such as ⟨zero⟩ and ⟨rosy⟩ sound as [ˈdʒiːro] and [ˈroːdʒiː] (the latter, especially in the North). This replacement is equally true for [Persian](/wiki/Persian_language "Persian language") and [Arabic](/wiki/Arabic "Arabic") loanwords into Hindi. The probable reason is the confusion created by the use of the Devanagari grapheme ⟨ज⟩ (for /dʒ/) with the [Nuqta](/wiki/Nuqta "Nuqta") to represent /z/ (as ⟨ज़⟩). A similar thing happens in other Indian languages like [Bengali](/wiki/Bengali_language "Bengali language"), with the letters for /dʒ/ (except Indian varieties of [Nepali](/wiki/Nepali_language "Nepali language") and [Marathi](/wiki/Marathi_language "Marathi language") where ⟨ज⟩ represents /[dz](/wiki/Voiced_alveolar_affricate "Voiced alveolar affricate")/) usually being used to represent /z/. This is common among people without formal English education. In [Telugu](/wiki/Telugu_language "Telugu language") (plus even in [Hindi](/wiki/Hindi "Hindi") and [Punjabi](/wiki/Punjabi_language "Punjabi language") to some extent) /z/ and /dʒ/ are allophones in some cases, so the words such as *fridge* /fɹɪdʒ/ become /friz/.
* In [Assamese](/wiki/Assamese_language "Assamese language"), /tʃ/ and /ʃ/ are pronounced as /s/; and /dʒ/ and /ʒ/ are pronounced as /z/. Retroflex and dental consonants are not present and only alveolar consonants are used unlike other Indian languages. Similar to [Bengali](/wiki/Bengali_language "Bengali language") and [Odia](/wiki/Odia_language "Odia language"), /v/ is pronounced as /bʱ/ and /β/ in [Assamese](/wiki/Assamese_language "Assamese language"). For example; change is pronounced as [sɛɪnz], vote is pronounced as [bʱot] and English is pronounced as [iŋlis].[[65]](#cite_note-Mahanta_2012_217–224-65)
* Again, in [Awadhi](/wiki/Awadhi_language "Awadhi language"), [Bhojpuri](/wiki/Bhojpuri_language "Bhojpuri language"), [Chhattisgarhi](/wiki/Chhattisgarhi_language "Chhattisgarhi language"), [Kannauji](/wiki/Kannauji_language "Kannauji language") and [Odia](/wiki/Odia_language "Odia language"), all instances of /ʃ/ are spoken like [s] (and [x] in [Assamese](/wiki/Assamese_language "Assamese language")), a phenomenon that is also apparent in their English. Exactly the opposite is seen for many [Bengalis](/wiki/Bengali_people "Bengali people").[[65]](#cite_note-Mahanta_2012_217–224-65)
* Inability to pronounce certain (especially word-initial) [consonant clusters](/wiki/Consonant_cluster "Consonant cluster") by people of rural backgrounds (as with some [Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_language "Spanish language"), [Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_language "Portuguese language") and [Persian](/wiki/Persian_language "Persian language") speakers). This is usually dealt with by [epenthesis](/wiki/Epenthesis "Epenthesis"). e.g., ⟨school⟩ /isˈkuːl/.
* Many Indians with lower exposure to English also may pronounce /f/ as an aspirated [voiceless bilabial plosive](/wiki/Voiceless_bilabial_plosive "Voiceless bilabial plosive") [pʰ]. Again in Hindi Devanagari the loaned /f/ from [Persian](/wiki/Persian_language "Persian language") and [Arabic](/wiki/Arabic "Arabic") is written by putting a dot beneath the grapheme for native [pʰ] ⟨फ⟩: ⟨फ़⟩. This substitution is rarer than that for [z], and in fact in many [Hindi](/wiki/Hindi "Hindi") and [Punjabi](/wiki/Punjabi_language "Punjabi language") words /f/ is used by native speakers instead of /pʰ/, or the two are used interchangeably.
* Some speakers of Indian English, unless conscious; do not use the [voiced postalveolar fricative](/wiki/Voiced_postalveolar_fricative "Voiced postalveolar fricative") (/ʒ/). Some Indians use /z/ (especially by [Hindi](/wiki/Hindi "Hindi") and [Punjabi](/wiki/Punjabi_language "Punjabi language") speakers) or /dʒ/ (basically by most of the Indians, who do not even have [z] phoneme) instead, e.g. ⟨treasure⟩ /ˈtrɛzəːr/,[[54]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWells1982628-54) and in some south Indian variants, with /ʃ/ as in ⟨shore⟩, e.g. ⟨treasure⟩ /ˈtrɛʃər/.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Spelling pronunciation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indian_English&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Spelling pronunciation")]

A number of distinctive features of Indian English are due to "[the vagaries of English spelling](/wiki/English_orthography "English orthography")".[[59]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWells1982629-59) Most Indian languages, unlike English, have a nearly [phonetic spelling](/wiki/Phonetic_spelling "Phonetic spelling"), so the spelling of a word is a highly reliable guide to its modern pronunciation. Indians' tendency to pronounce English phonetically as well can cause divergence from British English. This phenomenon is known as [spelling pronunciation](/wiki/Spelling_pronunciation "Spelling pronunciation").

* In words where the digraph ⟨gh⟩ represents a [voiced velar plosive](/wiki/Voiced_velar_plosive "Voiced velar plosive") (/ɡ/) in other accents, some Indian English speakers supply a murmured version [ɡʱ], for example ⟨ghost⟩ [ɡʱoːst]. No other accent of English admits this voiced aspiration.[[54]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWells1982628-54)
* Similarly, especially with Hindi speakers, the digraph ⟨wh⟩ may be aspirated as [ʋʱ] or [wʱ], resulting in realisations such as ⟨which⟩ [ʋʱɪtʃ], found in no other English accent (although some Scottish accents come close).[[66]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWells1982630-66) This is somewhat similar to the traditional distinction between ⟨wh⟩ and ⟨w⟩ present in English, however, wherein the former is /ʍ/, whilst the latter is /w/.
* In unstressed syllables, which speakers of American English would realise as a [schwa](/wiki/Schwa "Schwa"), speakers of Indian English would use the spelling vowel, making ⟨sanity⟩ sound as [ˈsæniti] instead of [ˈsænəti]. This trait is also present in other South Asian dialects ([Pakistani](/wiki/Pakistani_English "Pakistani English") and [Sri Lankan English](/wiki/Sri_Lankan_English "Sri Lankan English")), and common for many second-language European speakers of English.
* The word "of" is usually pronounced with a /f/ instead of a /v/ as in most other accents.[[59]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWells1982629-59)
* Use of [d] instead of [t] for the "-ed" ending of the past tense after voiceless consonants, for example "developed" may be [ˈdɛʋləpd] instead of RP /dɪˈvɛləpt/.[[54]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWells1982628-54)
* Use of [s] instead of [z] for the ⟨-s⟩ ending of the plural after voiced consonants, for example ⟨dogs⟩ may be [daɡs] instead of [dɒɡz].[[59]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWells1982629-59)
* Pronunciation of ⟨house⟩ as [haʊz] in both the noun and the verb, instead of [haʊs] as a noun and [haʊz] as a verb.
* Silent letters may be pronounced. For example, 'salmon' is usually pronounced with a distinct /l/.

### Supra-segmental features

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indian_English&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Supra-segmental features")]

English is a [stress-timed language](/wiki/Stress-timed_language "Stress-timed language"). Both syllable stress and [word stress](/wiki/Stress_(linguistics) "Stress (linguistics)") (where only certain words in a sentence or phrase are stressed) are important features of Received Pronunciation. Indian native languages are actually [syllable-timed languages](/wiki/Syllable-timed_language "Syllable-timed language"), like French. Indian-English speakers usually speak with a syllabic rhythm.[[67]](#cite_note-67) Further, in some Indian languages, stress is associated with a low pitch,[[68]](#cite_note-68) whereas in most English dialects, stressed syllables are generally pronounced with a higher pitch. Thus, when some Indian speakers speak, they appear to put the stress accents at the wrong syllables, or accentuate all the syllables of a long English word. Certain Indian accents possess a "sing-song" quality, a feature seen in a few English dialects of Britain, such as [Scouse](/wiki/Scouse "Scouse") and [Welsh English](/wiki/Welsh_English "Welsh English").[[69]](#cite_note-69)

## Numbering system

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indian_English&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Numbering system")]

The [Indian numbering system](/wiki/Indian_numbering_system "Indian numbering system") is preferred for digit grouping.[[70]](#cite_note-70) When written in words, or when spoken, numbers less than 100,000 are expressed just as they are in Standard English. Numbers including and beyond 100,000 are expressed in a subset of the Indian numbering system. Thus, the following scale is used:

| In digits (International system) | In digits (Indian system) | In words ([short scales](/wiki/Long_and_short_scales "Long and short scales")) | In words (Indian system) |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 10 | | ten | |
| 100 | | one hundred | |
| 1,000 | | one thousand | |
| 10,000 | | ten thousand | |
| 100,000 | 1,00,000 | one hundred thousand | one [lakh](/wiki/Lakh "Lakh") (from *lākh* लाख) |
| 1,000,000 | 10,00,000 | one million | ten [lakh](/wiki/Lakh "Lakh") |
| 10,000,000 | 1,00,00,000 | ten million | one [crore](/wiki/Crore "Crore") (from *karoṛ* करोड़) |
| 100,000,000 | 10,00,00,000 | one hundred million | ten [crore](/wiki/Crore "Crore") |
| 1,000,000,000 | 1,00,00,00,000 | one billion | one hundred [crore](/wiki/Crore "Crore") one [arab](/wiki/Arab_(number) "Arab (number)") (from *arab* अरब) |
| 10,000,000,000 | 10,00,00,00,000 | ten billion | one thousand [crore](/wiki/Crore "Crore") ten [arab](/wiki/Arab_(number) "Arab (number)") |
| 100,000,000,000 | 1,00,00,00,00,000 | one hundred billion | ten thousand [crore](/wiki/Crore "Crore") one [kharab](/wiki/Indian_numbering_system "Indian numbering system") (from *kharab* खरब) ([arab](/wiki/Arab_(number) "Arab (number)") and [kharab](/wiki/Indian_numbering_system "Indian numbering system") are not commonly used today) |

Larger numbers are generally expressed as multiples of the above (for example, one lakh crores for one [trillion](/wiki/Trillion_(short_scale) "Trillion (short scale)")).[[71]](#cite_note-71)[[72]](#cite_note-72)

## Vocabulary

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indian_English&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Vocabulary")]

Further information: [Glossary of the British Raj](/wiki/Glossary_of_the_British_Raj "Glossary of the British Raj")

Indian English includes many political, sociological, and administrative terms, such as [dharna](/wiki/Dharna "Dharna"), [hartal](/wiki/Hartal "Hartal"), [eve-teasing](/wiki/Eve-teasing "Eve-teasing"), [vote bank](/wiki/Votebank "Votebank"), [swaraj](/wiki/Swaraj "Swaraj"), [swadeshi](/wiki/Swadeshi_movement "Swadeshi movement"), [scheduled caste](/wiki/Scheduled_caste "Scheduled caste"), [scheduled tribe](/wiki/Scheduled_tribe "Scheduled tribe"), and [NRI](/wiki/Non-resident_Indian_and_person_of_Indian_origin "Non-resident Indian and person of Indian origin"). It incorporates some [Anglo-Indian](/wiki/Anglo_Indian "Anglo Indian") words such as [tiffin](/wiki/Tiffin "Tiffin"), [hill station](/wiki/Hill_station "Hill station"), [gymkhana](/wiki/Gymkhana "Gymkhana"), along with slang.[[73]](#cite_note-73)[[74]](#cite_note-74)

Indian English, like some other [World Englishes](/wiki/World_Englishes "World Englishes"), is notable for its treatment of English [mass](/wiki/Mass_noun "Mass noun") and [count nouns](/wiki/Count_noun "Count noun"). Words that are treated as mass nouns in native forms of English, such as *evidence*, *equipment*, or *training*, are frequently treated as count nouns in Indian English.[[75]](#cite_note-75)

Some examples of words and phrases unique to, or chiefly used in, standard written Indian English include:

* *ayye*, *aiye* ([interjection](/wiki/Interjection "Interjection")) (South India): ew.
* *ayyo*, *aiyo* (interjection) (South India): oh no, yikes.
* *brinjal* (noun): An [eggplant](/wiki/Eggplant "Eggplant")/aubergine
* *bus stand* (noun): A [bus station](/wiki/Bus_station "Bus station") (British English)
* *chain-snatching* (verb): To snatch a gold chain (or sometimes silver chain) from someone and run away, usually perpetrated by two or more criminals on a [motorbike](/wiki/Motorbike "Motorbike")/[moped](/wiki/Moped "Moped")/[scooter](/wiki/Scooter_(motorcycle) "Scooter (motorcycle)").
  + e.g. "Women are avoiding wearing gold chains due to the concerning rise in number of *chain-snatching* cases in many parts of the city."
* *cinema* (noun): A [movie](/wiki/Movie "Movie") or film
* *cinema hall* (noun): A [cinema](/wiki/Movie_theater "Movie theater") or [movie theatre](/wiki/Movie_theatre "Movie theatre")[[76]](#cite_note-76)
  + e.g. "*Cinema halls* in [Uttar Pradesh](/wiki/Uttar_Pradesh "Uttar Pradesh") will soon display the newly-unveiled logo for [Kumbh Mela](/wiki/Kumbh_Mela "Kumbh Mela"), right after [the national anthem](/wiki/Jana_Gana_Mana "Jana Gana Mana") is played" (*[The Times of India](/wiki/The_Times_of_India "The Times of India")*, 3 January 2018)[[77]](#cite_note-77)
* *[communalism](/wiki/Communalism_(South_Asia) "Communalism (South Asia)")*: The creation of hatred between different religions and ethnicities which cause [communal violence](/wiki/Communal_violence "Communal violence") between them. The term is usually used to describe the hatred spread by religious leaders and politicians which cause [Hindu–Muslim riots](/wiki/Hindu%E2%80%93Muslim_riots "Hindu–Muslim riots").
* *[desi](/wiki/Desi "Desi")*: South Asian, Indian.
* *[do the needful](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/do_the_needful "wikt:do the needful")*: To do that which is necessary or required, with the respectful implication that the other party is trusted to understand what needs doing without being given detailed instructions.
  + e.g. "When asked if the [UP](/wiki/Uttar_Pradesh "Uttar Pradesh") government could reduce [Value Added Tax](/wiki/Value-added_tax "Value-added tax") (VAT) on petro-products to bring down prices, the [CM](/wiki/List_of_chief_ministers_of_Uttar_Pradesh "List of chief ministers of Uttar Pradesh") said that the [state government](/wiki/Government_of_Uttar_Pradesh "Government of Uttar Pradesh") was aware of the situation and will *do the needful*." (2018 *[The Pioneer](/wiki/The_Pioneer_(India) "The Pioneer (India)")*)[[78]](#cite_note-78)
* *English-knowing* ([adjective](/wiki/Adjective "Adjective")): Of a person or group of people that uses or speaks English.
  + e.g. "The official and Service atmosphere ... set the tone for almost all [Indian middle-class](/wiki/Indian_middle_class "Indian middle class") life, especially the *English-knowing* [intelligentsia](/wiki/Intelligentsia "Intelligentsia")." (*[Toward Freedom](/wiki/An_Autobiography_(Nehru) "An Autobiography (Nehru)")* vii. 40, [J. Nehru](/wiki/Jawaharlal_Nehru "Jawaharlal Nehru"), 1941)[[79]](#cite_note-79)
* *foreign-returned* (adjective): Of a person or group of people who has returned home after living abroad for a while[[80]](#cite_note-80)
* *freeship* (noun): A studentship or [scholarship](/wiki/Scholarship "Scholarship").[[81]](#cite_note-81)
  + e.g. "Two permanent *freeships*, each tenable for one year and one of which is for the second and the other for the third year class." (*The Medical Reporter* (Calcutta) 57/1, 1 February 1893)
  + e.g. "Private institutions can only develop if they are allowed to charge reasonable fees, while also providing need based *freeships* and scholarships for a certain percentage of students." (*[The Economic Times](/wiki/The_Economic_Times "The Economic Times")* (Nexis), 12 October 2006)[[82]](#cite_note-82)
* *hartal* (noun): A strike, protest.
* *hotel* (noun): A [restaurant](/wiki/Restaurant "Restaurant") or [café](/wiki/Caf%C3%A9 "Café").
  + e.g. "A group of four friends had gone to have dinner at a roadside *hotel*." (*Statesman* (Calcutta), 10 February 1999 (Midweek section) 4/3)[[83]](#cite_note-83)
* *is it so:* Oh really?
* *it will be*: This is how much it is (quantifying something)[*[dubious](/wiki/Wikipedia:Disputed_statement "Wikipedia:Disputed statement") – [discuss](/wiki/Talk:Indian_English#Dubious "Talk:Indian English")*].
  + e.g. Q: "How much is this?" A: "It will be two hundred and seventy rupees."
* *kindly adjust*: Used to acknowledge and apologise for something that causes problems or difficulties and ask people to accept and adapt to the situation, or used to apologise for causing inconvenience.
  + e.g. "The store will be closed this afternoon due to staffing shortages. *Kindly adjust*."
  + e.g.: When asking someone to move along so you can sit down. *"*I would like to sit down, sir. *Kindly adjust*."[[84]](#cite_note-84)
* *matrimonial* (noun): [Advertisements](/wiki/Advertising "Advertising") in a [newspaper](/wiki/Newspaper "Newspaper") for the purpose of finding a marriageable partner.
  + e.g. "When I have a job I'll have to begin a whole new search for my better half ... Back to the newspaper *matrimonials* on Sundays." (*Statesman* (Calcutta), 10 February 1999 (Midweek section) 4/3)[[85]](#cite_note-85)
* *na* ([interjection](/wiki/Interjection "Interjection")) (North India): "isn't it?"
  + e.g. "That place is quite far na?"
* *office boy:* Usually a person employed to do less important and menial jobs in a business office (such as a messenger, copier maintenance, [Chaiwala](/wiki/Chaiwala "Chaiwala"), etc.). Often resides or spends their working time in a special service space in the office, behind the front desk or in the pantry.
* *out of station*: used for saying that someone is away.[[86]](#cite_note-86) This phrase has its origins in the posting of army officers to particular "stations" during the days of the [East India Company](/wiki/East_India_Company "East India Company").
* *pass*(*ing*) *out* (phrase): Graduate from school/college or complete a course at an institution.[[87]](#cite_note-:1-87)
  + e.g. "I passed out of college in 2007."[[87]](#cite_note-:1-87)
  + e.g. "I passed out of my school aged 17."
* *petrol pump / petrol bunk* (used in some parts of south India; noun): A [petrol station](/wiki/Filling_station "Filling station") (British English), gas station (American English)
* *prepone* (verb): To bring (something) forward to an earlier date or time.[[88]](#cite_note-88)
  + e.g. "The meeting has been *preponed* due to a change in the schedule."
* *pressperson* (noun, frequently as a single word): A newspaper journalist, a reporter, a member of the press.
  + e.g. "The [Prime Minister](/wiki/Atal_Bihari_Vajpayee "Atal Bihari Vajpayee") greeted the *presspersons* with a '[namaskar](/wiki/Namaskar "Namaskar")' [customary Hindu greeting] and a broad smile." (*The Hindu* (Nexis), 20 June 2001)[[89]](#cite_note-89)
* *ragging* (noun): [bullying](/wiki/Bullying "Bullying"), [fagging](/wiki/Fagging "Fagging")
* *redressal* (noun): Redress
  + e.g. "There is an urgent need for setting up an independent authority for *redressal* of telecom consumer complaints." (*Statesman* (India) (Nexis), 2 April 1998)
  + e.g. "Where does he go for the *redressal* of his genuine grievances?" (*[Sunday Times of India](/wiki/Sunday_Times_of_India "Sunday Times of India")*, 15 September 2002 8/4)[[90]](#cite_note-90)
* *revert* ([verb](/wiki/Verb "Verb")): To report back with information.
  + e.g. "Please *revert* with the required documentation."
* *road junction/circle* (noun): a [crossroad](/wiki/Intersection_(road) "Intersection (road)") (British English), [intersection](/wiki/Intersection_(road) "Intersection (road)") (American English)
* *tiffin*: lunch, snack.
* *updation*: The act of updating.
* *upgradation* (noun): The enhancement or upgrading of status, value or level of something.
  + e.g. "Our Company lays great stress on technical training and knowledge *upgradation*." (*Business India*, 8 September 1986 153/1 (advert))[[91]](#cite_note-91)
* *[votebank](/wiki/Votebank "Votebank")*: A [bloc](/wiki/Voting_bloc "Voting bloc") of [voters](/wiki/Voting "Voting") from a single [community](/wiki/Community "Community") or a group of communities who always back a certain [candidate](/wiki/Candidate "Candidate") or [political party](/wiki/Political_party "Political party") for bribes and/or employment favours given by the particular party.

## Spelling

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indian_English&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Spelling")]

Spelling practices in Indian English generally follow the [British style](/wiki/American_and_British_English_spelling_differences "American and British English spelling differences"), e.g., using *travelling*, *litre*, *practise* (as a verb), *anaesthesia*, *fulfil*, *catalogue*, *realise* and *colour*, rather than the American style.[[92]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESailaja200983,_115-92)

## Dictionaries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indian_English&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Dictionaries")]

The most famous dictionary of Indian English is [Yule](/wiki/Henry_Yule "Henry Yule") and [Brunell](/wiki/Arthur_Coke_Burnell "Arthur Coke Burnell")'s *[Hobson-Jobson](/wiki/Hobson-Jobson "Hobson-Jobson")*, originally published in 1886 with an expanded edition edited by [William Crooke](/wiki/William_Crooke "William Crooke") in 1903, widely available in reprint since the 1960s.

Numerous other dictionaries ostensibly covering Indian English, though for the most part being merely collections of administratively-useful words from local languages, include (chronologically): [Rousseau](/wiki/Samuel_Rousseau_(orientalist) "Samuel Rousseau (orientalist)") *A Dictionary of Words used in the East Indies* (1804), [Wilkins](/wiki/Charles_Wilkins "Charles Wilkins") *Glossary to the Fifth Report* (1813), [Stocqueler](/wiki/Joachim_Hayward_Stocqueler "Joachim Hayward Stocqueler") *The Oriental Interpreter and Treasury of East Indian Knowledge* (1844), [Elliot](/wiki/Henry_Miers_Elliot "Henry Miers Elliot") *A Supplement to the Glossary of Indian Terms: A–J* (1845), [Brown](/wiki/Charles_Philip_Brown "Charles Philip Brown") *The Zillah Dictionary in the Roman Character* (1852), [Carnegy](/w/index.php?title=Patrick_Carnegy_(writer)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Patrick Carnegy (writer) (page does not exist)") *Kutcherry Technicalities* (1853) and its second edition *Kachahri Technicalities* (1877), [Wilson](/wiki/Horace_Hayman_Wilson "Horace Hayman Wilson") *Glossary of Judicial and Revenue Terms* (1855), [Giles](/wiki/Herbert_Giles "Herbert Giles") *A Glossary of Reference, on Subjects connected with the Far East* (1878), [Whitworth](/w/index.php?title=George_Clifford_Whitworth&action=edit&redlink=1 "George Clifford Whitworth (page does not exist)") *Anglo-Indian Dictionary* (1885), [Temple](/w/index.php?title=George_Temple_(writer)&action=edit&redlink=1 "George Temple (writer) (page does not exist)") *A Glossary of Indian Terms relating to Religion, Customs, Government, Land* (1897), and [Crooke](/wiki/William_Crooke "William Crooke") *Things India: Being Discursive Notes on Various Subjects connected with India* (1906).

The first dictionary of Indian English to be published after independence was Hawkins *Common Indian Words in English* (1984). Other efforts include (chronologically): Lewis *Sahibs, Nabobs and Boxwallahs* (1991), Muthiah *Words in Indian English* (1991), Sengupta's Indian English supplement to the *Oxford Advanced Learner's Dictionary* (1996) and [Hankin](/wiki/Nigel_Hankin "Nigel Hankin") *Hanklyn-Janklin* (2003). Nihalani et al. *Indian and British English: A Handbook of Usage and Pronunciation* (2004) delineates how Indian English differs from British English for a large number of specific lexical items. The [Macmillan](/wiki/Macmillan_Publishers "Macmillan Publishers") publishing company also produced a range of synchronic general dictionaries for the Indian market, such as the *Macmillan Comprehensive Dictionary* (2006).

The most recent dictionary is Carls *A Dictionary of Indian English, with a Supplement on Word-formation Patterns* (2017).[*[needs update](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*]
